seo.title: Llama Stack Guardrails for Healthcare
seo.description: Compliance guide for Llama Stack agents in healthcare. Meet HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Healthcare teams deploying Llama Stack face immense risks due to stringent regulations like HIPAA and FDA 21 CFR Part 11, where mishandling PHI or unauditable AI decisions can lead to patient harm and fines up to $1.5M per violation. Llama Stack's safety shields, agentic loops, tools, and memory banks introduce failure modes like false positives on benign medical queries and hallucinated document retrievals that compromise compliance. Vex provides essential post-processing guardrails via vex.verify() to ensure every agent turn meets healthcare standards without disrupting workflow.

complianceRequirements.description: Protects PHI from unauthorized disclosure; requires de-identification before LLM processing and audit trails for all data access.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex verifies agent responses for PHI leakage, enforces de-identification schemas, and logs verifiable audit trails for every Llama Stack turn.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates electronic records and signatures be trustworthy, auditable, and tamper-proof for AI-assisted clinical decisions.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex wraps Llama Stack responses with cryptographic verification, ensuring decision provenance and schema conformance for regulatory validation.

complianceRequirements.description: Strengthens HIPAA with breach notifications and stricter penalties; patient data cannot leave secure environments.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex runtime checks block data exfiltration attempts in tool calls and flags boundary-crossing memory retrievals.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires demonstrated controls for security, availability, and confidentiality over 6+ months in cloud AI deployments.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex provides continuous monitoring and compliance attestations for Llama Stack agent loops, with failure mode dashboards.

industryRisks.name: PHI Leakage via Hallucinated Memory Retrieval
industryRisks.description: Llama Stack memory bank incorrectly associates patient documents, exposing PHI in responses.
industryRisks.example: Agent retrieves 'John Doe MRI' instead of de-identified template, violating HIPAA.

industryRisks.name: Safety Shield False Positives on Medical Terms
industryRisks.description: Benign anatomical terms trigger filtering, blocking legitimate clinical queries.
industryRisks.example: 'Mitral valve prolapse' flagged as harmful content, delaying diagnosis.

industryRisks.name: Unauditable Agentic Loop Exits
industryRisks.description: Turn limits exceeded without resolution, leaving clinical decisions unresolved.
industryRisks.example: Patient triage agent loops indefinitely on ambiguous symptoms.

industryRisks.name: Tool Schema Violations in EHR Integration
industryRisks.description: Tool responses fail expected schemas, corrupting downstream clinical systems.
industryRisks.example: EHR query returns malformed JSON, crashing hospital dashboard.

implementation.title: PHI De-identification Wrapper
implementation.description: Wrap Llama Stack agent responses to verify PHI redaction before clinical use.
implementation.code:
import vex
from llama_stack_client import LlamaStackAgent

agent = LlamaStackAgent()

@vex.verify(
    rules=[
        "response contains no identifiable PHI",
        "all patient names redacted",
        "dates shifted per HIPAA safe harbor"
    ],
    sensitivity="critical"
)
def safe_clinical_response(prompt):
    response = agent.chat(prompt)
    return response

# Usage
result = safe_clinical_response("Summarize patient chart: John Doe, DOB 1980-05-15")

implementation.title: Agent Loop Audit Guardrail
implementation.description: Ensure Llama Stack agentic loops produce auditable resolutions for FDA 21 CFR Part 11.
implementation.code:
import vex
from llama_stack_client import LlamaStackAgent

agent = LlamaStackAgent(max_turns=10)

@vex.verify(
    rules=[
        "agent resolved with clear decision",
        "turn limit not exceeded",
        "decision includes confidence score",
        "audit trail complete"
    ],
    audit=True
)
def auditable_triage_loop(symptoms):
    conversation = agent.converse(symptoms)
    return {
        "diagnosis": conversation.final_decision,
        "confidence": conversation.confidence,
        "turns": len(conversation.history)
    }

# Usage
triage = auditable_triage_loop("chest pain, age 55, smoker")

implementation.title: Tool Schema Compliance Check
implementation.description: Validate Llama Stack tool responses conform to EHR integration schemas.
implementation.code:
import vex
import json
from llama_stack_client import LlamaStackAgent

agent = LlamaStackAgent()

@vex.verify(
    rules=[
        "response is valid JSON",
        "contains required fields: mrn, diagnosis, confidence",
        "no PHI in non-deidentified fields",
        "timestamp present"
    ],
    schema="ehr_response_schema.json"
)
def safe_ehr_tool_call(query):
    tool_response = agent.call_tool("ehr_lookup", {"query": query})
    return json.loads(tool_response)

# Usage
ehr_data = safe_ehr_tool_call("recent labs for patient")

caseStudyScenario.heading: Llama Stack in Healthcare: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
City General Hospital deployed Llama Stack agents for real-time patient triage and EHR summarization. The system processed 500+ daily queries, pulling from memory banks of de-identified clinical guidelines and calling EHR tools for lab results. However, safety shields began flagging benign terms like 'aortic aneurysm' as violent content, while memory retrieval hallucinated associations between guideline documents and live patient MRNs. Agent loops frequently exceeded turn limits without resolution during complex cases, and tool responses occasionally returned malformed JSON that crashed the hospital dashboard. During a night shift, an unaudited loop failed to triage a STEMI patient, delaying cath lab activation by 45 minutes.

The compliance team discovered these failures only after a HIPAA audit flagged PHI leakage in cached memory responses. FDA inspectors questioned the auditability of AI-assisted decisions, and SOC 2 recertification was jeopardized by missing controls over agent behavior.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: After implementing Vex guardrails wrapping every Llama Stack turn, false positives dropped 98%, with healthcare-specific allowlists for medical terminology. Agent loops now auto-fail into human review with complete audit trails, satisfying 21 CFR Part 11. Tool schema violations were eliminated, preventing dashboard crashes. The STEMI incident was prevented in simulation testing, and the HIPAA audit passed with zero findings. Vex dashboards provided regulators with real-time compliance attestations, securing SOC 2 Type II recertification and avoiding $2.3M in potential fines.
